title: AI is a Gamechanger for TLA+ Users
summary: Comprehensive analysis of how LLMs (specifically Claude 3.7) assist with TLA+ formal specification. LLMs excel at tedious tasks: fixing syntax errors, explaining error traces, and handling boilerplate like UNCHANGED clauses. Azure used LLMs to derive TLA+ specs from codebases and found production bugs.
insights: LLMs are 'specification force multipliers' for routine TLA+ tasks but not strategic thinking. The VSCode TLA+ extension now has agent actions for SANY error fixing. Key limitation: LLMs sometimes suggest 'fixes' that declare bugs acceptable rather than actually fixing them.

title: Enough AI Copilots, We Need AI HUDs
summary: Argues that chat-based AI interfaces are limiting—developers need ambient awareness of AI insights while working, not separate conversation contexts. Proposes 'AI HUDs' that integrate suggestions directly into the development environment without requiring explicit prompts.
insights: Current chat interfaces create context switching costs. HUDs would provide passive, contextual assistance—like inline suggestions for refactoring or performance improvements. The goal is reducing friction between human intent and AI assistance.

title: Everything I Hate About React, I Really Hate About JavaScript
summary: Traces React's memoization complexity (useCallback, useMemo, useEffect dependency arrays) to JavaScript's reference equality semantics. Structural equality would eliminate the 'memoization cascade' that makes React code verbose and error-prone.
insights: Reference equality ([1,2,3] === [1,2,3] is false) forces React to re-render when objects change identity. Cloudflare's 2024 outage was caused by useEffect running repeatedly due to object recreation in dependency arrays. The React Compiler auto-inserts memoization but has unpredictable limitations.

title: Rewriting pycparser with the help of an LLM
summary: Case study of collaborating with Codex to rewrite pycparser (20M+ daily PyPI downloads) from PLY-based parsing to hand-written recursive-descent. A real-world example of using AI coding agents on production infrastructure code.
insights: Demonstrates that LLM agents can handle complex refactoring of mature, widely-used libraries when guided by an expert. The move from parser generators to hand-written recursive descent shows LLMs can help with architectural transitions, not just boilerplate. Domain expertise + LLM = powerful combination.

title: Thoughts on AI Progress (Dec 2025)
summary: Nuanced critique of current AI progress timelines. Heavy reliance on RL with verifiable outcomes suggests models lack true generalization. 'Models keep getting more impressive at the rate short timelines people predict, but more useful at the rate long timelines people predict.'
insights: Current models are like Student 1 who practices 10,000 hours for competitive programming vs Student 2 who practiced 100 hours but generalizes better. The robotics problem is fundamentally an algorithms problem, not hardware. Most compute will shift to inference, not training.

title: Proving What's Possible
summary: Explores how formal methods can prove what systems *can* do, not just what they can't. Demonstrates using TLA+ and TLC to exhaustively search for solutions to constraint satisfaction problems like n-queens and edge-matching puzzles.
insights: TLA+ model checker TLC can find solutions to NP-complete constraint problems by encoding them as state spaces. While not the most efficient approach, it reveals that formal specification tools have broader applicability than just verification.

title: The Liskov Substitution Principle Does More Than You Think
summary: Revisits LSP beyond the common 'inheritance' understanding—it's really about behavioral subtyping where any subtype must satisfy the same properties as its supertype. Connects this to formal methods and design by contract.
insights: LSP is fundamentally about preserving properties, not just method signatures. When combined with design by contract (DbC), it becomes a powerful verification tool. Behavioral subtyping prevents subtle bugs in polymorphic code and enables more robust abstraction hierarchies.

title: The GDB JIT Interface
summary: Comprehensive guide to enabling GDB debugging for JIT-compiled code. Covers both the traditional ELF/DWARF-based interface and the newer custom debug reader interface. Includes implementation examples from V8, JavaScriptCore, LuaJIT, .NET.
insights: Traditional interface requires generating ELF objects in memory; newer interface allows custom binary formats with reader plugins. O(n²) behavior in linked list registration; GC requires special handling for code movement.

title: ZJIT Available in Ruby 4.0
summary: Announcement of ZJIT shipping in Ruby 4.0 with detailed progress report. Covers side-exits, register spilling, C ABI calls, and a concurrent garbage collector. Project took 2 years and ~$300K in engineering time.
insights: Ruby is now the only major high-level language with a concurrent GC. The team set a target of 1.5x speedup on headline benchmarks (yjit-bench) and hit 1.55x on railsbench. Keeping the compiler 'dumb' (no LICM, DCE, GVN, GCM) actually improved performance through better instruction cache locality.

title: Code Like a Surgeon
summary: Proposes treating AI-assisted coding like surgery—meticulous preparation, clear goals, and careful execution. The author contrasts 'vibe coding' (AI generates, human hopes) with intentional AI collaboration where the human maintains control and understanding.
insights: Surgeons don't 'vibe operate'—they plan, prepare, and execute with precision. Similarly, AI-assisted development works best when developers have clear mental models and use AI as a tool, not a replacement for thinking.

title: Ilya Sutskever Interview
summary: Ilya discusses SSI's strategy, limitations of pre-training, and how to improve model generalization. Critiques current RL approach where teams produce endless custom environments—models that over-train on specific domains don't generalize to become better overall.
insights: Pre-training is 'very difficult to reason about because it's so hard to understand what all the data is doing.' The competitive programming analogy: over-trained models are like Student 1 with 10,000 hours who may lose to Student 2 with 100 hours in actual career outcomes.

title: Refinement without Specification
summary: Discusses 'refinement'—implementing abstract specifications with concrete code. Argues the real value lies in the thinking process, even without a formal spec. Shows how asking 'what would a specification look like?' catches bugs before they're written.
insights: Refinement thinking helps identify hidden assumptions and edge cases. You don't need to write full formal specs to benefit—just asking specification-level questions reveals gaps. Demonstrated through a case study of a bug in a simple counter implementation.

title: Stevens: A Hackable AI Assistant
summary: Documents building a personal AI assistant using minimal infrastructure: one SQLite table, cron jobs, and simple heuristics. The system proactively surfaces relevant information without requiring explicit queries.
insights: AI assistants don't need complex architectures to be useful. Simple, hackable systems can provide significant value through proactive, contextual information delivery. The key is designing for personal workflows rather than generic capabilities.

title: Hot Reloading is Better Than SwiftUI Previews
summary: Tutorial on implementing dynamic library hot reloading for SwiftUI apps without Xcode. Demonstrates using dlopen/dlsym to load Swift code dynamically, enabling faster iteration cycles than Xcode Previews while preserving real app state.
insights: Uses @_cdecl attribute to export Swift functions as C symbols, avoiding name mangling. Technique involves creating a host app that loads UI from a dynamic library, with state preservation across reloads. File modification time checks prevent unnecessary reloads.

title: Automatic Programming
summary: Redis creator distinguishes between 'vibe coding' (hands-off AI generation) and 'automatic programming' (human-guided AI development with ownership). When developers deeply engage with AI—understanding architecture and shaping decisions—the result is fundamentally different.
insights: The distinction between 'vibe coding' (AI generates, human reports bugs) vs 'automatic programming' (human guides, AI assists) is crucial for professional developers. Human vision and ownership: 'I'm a programmer, and I use automatic programming.'

title: Coding Agents as the New Compilers
summary: Frames AI coding agents as the next evolution of compilers—transforming high-level intent into executable code. Argues that just as compilers made assembly obsolete for most developers, AI agents will raise the abstraction level again.
insights: Historical pattern: each layer of abstraction (assembly → C → managed languages → AI agents) expands who can build software. The role of developers shifts from implementation details to architecture and intent specification.

title: Error Driven Development
summary: Argues that compiled languages with strong type systems enable faster development cycles than interpreted languages because type checking errors appear instantly in the IDE, whereas runtime errors require executing the code.
insights: Type checking is dramatically faster than codegen; exhaustive match checking on enums makes adding variants trivial; types serve as enforced documentation that cannot be ignored and stays up-to-date during dependency upgrades.

title: My Obvious Syncing Strategy
summary: Documents an event-based architecture for local-first web app syncing across multiple devices. Uses timestamped events stored per-device, k-way merge for ordering, and Supabase realtime for instant cross-device updates with OPFS for offline storage.
insights: Event-based architecture (similar to rollback netcode) enables conflict-free merging; storing events per-device in a HashMap<device_id, OrdSet<event>> allows efficient sync; critical to handle the three-representation problem (memory, disk, cloud) carefully.

title: Notes on Space GPUs
summary: Explores feasibility of orbital data centers for AI compute. Space offers 5x better solar efficiency and no batteries, but GPUs are 70% of datacenter costs vs only 15% for energy. To put 100GW in orbit would require ~10,000 Starship launches per year.
insights: Specific power budget (W/kg launched) is the critical constraint. At ~85 W/kg system-level efficiency, you'd need one Starship launch every hour to achieve 100GW in orbit. Radiation less problematic than expected; real challenge is servicing—GPUs fail every ~3 hours in large clusters on Earth.

title: Opinions About Parsing and Grammars
summary: Simon Tatham (PuTTY author) shares opinions on parsing. Discusses why hand-written recursive descent often beats parser generators in practice, the value of ambiguous grammars with explicit precedence, and when different parsing approaches make sense.
insights: Hand-written parsers offer better error messages and more flexible recovery than generated ones. Explicit precedence rules in ambiguous grammars are often clearer than unambiguous grammar gymnastics. Good parsing is about user experience (error messages) not just mathematical correctness.
